And, you know, I found myself giving the same answer that I always give on that, which is the most effective thing you need to do is you need to go upstream and you need to have more people coming out of college with computer science degrees who are, you know, skilled and qualified and trained, right, and mentored to be able to participate in the industry.
1:14:12
And, you know, you and I, Ben, both came out of state schools, you know, with computer science programs, you know, where we were able to then have the careers we've had. I find myself answering the question saying, well, we need more computer science graduates from every group.
1:14:29
But in the back of my head, I was like, it's going to be impossible to do that because none of these places are going to be able to afford to actually have the compute resources to be able to actually have AI programs in the future.
1:14:39
And so, like, you know, maybe the government can fix this by just dumping a ton of money on top of these universities. And maybe that's what will happen. And, you know, the current political environment seems like maybe it's not quite feasible for a variety of reasons.
1:14:52
And then the other approach would be a decentralized approach, would be a blockchain-based approach. that everybody could participate in if that were something that the government were willing to support, which right now it's not.
1:15:04
And so I think there's a really, really, really, really central, important, vital issue here that I think is being glossed over by a lot of people that I think should really be looked at.
1:15:14
Yeah, no, I think it's absolutely critical. And this is, you know, again, kind of going back to our original thing, like it's so important to the country being what, you know, America being what America should be to get these issues right.
1:15:29
And we're definitely in danger of that not happening, you know, because, you know, look, I think people are taking much too narrow view of some of these technologies and not understanding their full capabilities. And, you know, we get into, oh, the AI could, you know, say something racist. Therefore, we won't cure cancer.
1:15:47
I mean, like we're getting into that kind of dumb thing. And, you know, we need to have a tech forward kind of solution to some of these things and then the right regulatory approach to kind of make the whole environment work.
1:16:03
So let's go to the next phase of this now, which is the sort of global implications. So I'm gonna conjoin two different topics here, but I'm gonna do it on purpose. So Michael Frank Martin asks, what could the US do to position itself as the global leader of open source software?
1:16:16
Do you see any specific legislation or regulatory constraints that are hampering the development of open source projects? Arda asks similar question, what would an ideal AI policy for open-source software models looks like? Then Sarah Holmes asks the China question.
1:16:30
Do you think we will end up with two AI tech stacks, the Western China and ultimately companies will have to pick one side and stay on it?
1:16:36
And so, look, I would say that this is where you get to like the really, really big geopolitical long-term issue, which is basically my understanding of things is, is, is sort of as follows, which is, you know, basically the, the, for a variety of reasons, technological development in the West is being centralized into the United States, you know, with, with, you know, some in Canada and some in Europe, although, you know, quite frankly, a lot of, a lot of the best Canadian and, and you know, European, you know, tech founders are coming to Silicon Valley, you know, Yann LeCun, you know, teach,
1:17:02
Yann LeCun is a hero in France, teaches at NYU and works at Meta, both of which are American institutions. And so they're sort of an American or let's say American plus European kind of tech vanguard wedge in the world. And then there's China. And really, it's actually quite a bipolar situation.
1:17:22
I would say the dreams of tech being fully democratized and spreading throughout the world have been realized for sure on the use side, but not nearly as much on the entrepreneurship side or the invention side.
1:17:34
And again, immigration being a great virtue, but for the countries that are the beneficiaries of immigration, the other side of that is other countries are going to be less competitive because they're best and brightest moving to the US. So anyway, so we are in a bipolar tech world. It is primarily a bipolar tech world.
1:17:52
It's primarily the US and China. This is not the first time we have been in a bipolar world involving geopolitics and technology. The US and China have two very different systems. The Chinese system has all of the virtues and downsides of being centralized. The US system has all the virtues and downsides of being more decentralized.
1:18:16
There is a very different set of views of the two systems on how society should be ordered and what freedom means and what people should be able to do and not do.
1:18:27
And then look, both the US and China have visions of global supremacy and visions of basically care and agendas and programs to carry forward their points of view on the technology of AI and on the societal implications of AI throughout the world. And so, you know, there is this Cold War.
1:18:45
Oh, and then the other thing is just in D.C., it's just crystal clear that there's this now dynamic happening where Republicans and Democrats right now are trying to leapfrog each other every day on being more anti-China.
1:18:57
And so, you know, our friend Neil Ferguson is using the term, I think, Cold War 2.0, like we're in, whether we want to or not, like we're in Cold War 2.0, like we're in a dynamic similar to the one was with the USSR 30, 40, 50 years ago.
1:19:12
To Sarah Holmes' question, it's 100 percent going to be the case. There are two AI tech stacks and there are two AI governance models and there are two AI deployment systems.
1:19:23
There are two ways in which AI dovetails in everything from surveillance to smart cities, to transportation, self-driving cars, drones, who controls what, who gets access to what, who sees what. The degree by the way to which AI is used as a method for population control. There are very different visions. And these are national visions and global visions.
1:19:41
And there's a very big competition developing. And, you know, it certainly looks to me like there's going to be a winner and a loser. I think it's overwhelmingly in our best interest for the U.S. to be the winner. For the U.S. winner, we have to lean into our strengths.
1:19:57
And the downside of our system is that we are not as well organized and orchestrated top-down as China is. The upside of our system, at least remarkably, is that we're able to benefit from decentralization. We're able to benefit from competition, from a market economy, from a private sector.
1:20:14
where we're able to basically have a much larger number of smart people making lots of small decisions to be able to get to good outcomes as opposed to having a dictatorial system in which there's a small number of people trying to make decisions.
1:20:26
I mean, look, and this is how we won the Cold War against Russia, is our decentralized system just worked better economically, technologically, and ultimately militarily than the Soviet centralized system. And so it just seems fairly obvious to me that we have to lean into our strengths. We better lean into our strengths.
1:20:41
Because if we think we're just going to be like another version of a centralized system, but without all the advantages that China has with having a more centralized system, that just seems like a bad formula. So, yeah, let me pause there and, Ben, see what you think.
1:20:52
Yeah, I know, for sure. I think that'd be disastrous. And I think this is why it's so clear that if there's one, you know, to answer the question, if there's one regulatory policy that we would enact that would ensure America's competitiveness, it would be open source.
1:21:08
And the reason being that, as you said, this enables the largest number of participants to kind of contribute to A.I., to innovate, to come up with novel solutions, and so forth. And I think that you're right. What's going to happen in China is they're going to pick one because they can.
1:21:26
And they're going to kind of drive all their wood behind that arrow in a way that we could never do because we just don't work that way. And they're going to impose that on their society and try and impose it on the world.
1:21:39
And our best counter to that is to put it in the hands of all of our smart people. We have so many smart people from all over the world. As we like to say, diversity is our strength. We've got this tremendous different points of view, different kinds of people in our country.
1:21:58
And the more that we can enable them, the more likely we'll be competitive. And I'll give you a tremendous example of this. You know, I think if you go back to 2017 and you read any, you know, foreign policy magazine, et cetera, there wasn't a single one that didn't say China was ahead in AI.
1:22:17
They have more patents. They have more students going to universities. They're ahead in AI. They're ahead in AI. Like, we're behind in AI. And then, you know, Chad GPT comes out and goes, oh, I guess we're not behind in AI. We're ahead in AI.
1:22:30
And the truth of it was what China was ahead on was in integrating AI into the government, their one AI into their government in that way. And, you know, look, we're working on doing a better job of that with American dynamism, but we're never going to be good at that model.
1:22:49
You know, that's the model that they're going to be great at. And we have to be great at our model. And if we start limiting that, outlawing startups and outlawing anybody but the big companies from developing AI and all that kind of thing,
1:23:00
uh we'll definitely shoot ourselves in the foot i would say related or like another kind of important point i think in kind of the safety of the world um is you know when you talk about two ais
1:23:14
That's like two AI stacks, perhaps, but it's very important that countries that are in America, that are in China can align AI to their values. And I'll just give you kind of one really important example, which, you know, like I've been spending a lot of time in the Middle East.
1:23:33
And if you look at the kind of history of a country like Saudi Arabia, they're coming from a world of fundamentalism and a kind of set of values that they're trying to modernize. And they've done tremendous things with women's rights and so forth.
1:23:50
But, you know, look, there's still the fact that they've got, you know, people who don't want to go to that future so fast, and they need to preserve some of their history in order to not have a revolution or extreme violence and so forth.
1:24:06
And, yeah, we're seeing al-Qaeda re-spark up in Afghanistan and all these kinds of things, which are, by the way, al-Qaeda's real enemy of modern Saudi, just as much as they're an enemy of America. And so if Saudi can't align an AI to the current Saudi values, they could literally spark a revolution in their country.
1:24:29
And so it's very important that as we have technology that we develop, that it not be totally proprietary clone source, that it be kind of modifiable by our allies who need to kind of progress at their pace to keep their kind of country safe and keep us safe in doing so.
1:24:48
And so this has got great geopolitical ramifications, what we do here. Like, if we go to the China model that Google and Microsoft are advocating for, this Chinese model of only a few can control AI, we're going to be in big trouble.
1:25:05
Yeah, and then I just want to close on the open source point because it's so critical. So this is where, you know, I would say I get extremely irate at the idea of closing down open source, which, you know, people in a number of these people are lobbying for very actively.
1:25:17
By the way, I'm going to name one more name. We even have VCs lobbying to outlaw open source, which I find to just be completely staggering and impolite.
1:25:27
Vinod?
1:25:28
Vinod. So Vinod Khosla, who is, this is just incredible to me, he's the founder of Sun Microsystems, which was in many ways a company built on open source, built on open source Unix out of Berkeley, and then itself built a lot of open source, critical open source.
1:25:40
And then of course, you know, it was the .com, which of course, you know, the internet was all built on open source. And Vinod has been lobbying to ban open source AI. And by the way, he denies that he's been doing this, but I saw him with my own eyes when the U.S.
1:25:53
Congressional China Committee came to Stanford. I was in the meeting where he was with, you know, 30, 20 or 30 congressmen and lobbying actively for this. And so I've seen him do it myself. And, you know, look, he's got a big stake in OpenAI. You know, maybe it's financial self-interest.
1:26:08
By the way, maybe he's a true believer in the dangers.
1:26:10
But in any event. Well, I think he proved on Twitter he was not a true believer in the dangers. I'll get into that. I'll explain that.
1:26:18
Yeah. So, yeah, I mean, even within little tech, even within the startup world, we are not uniform in this. And I think that's extremely dangerous. Look, open source, like what is open source software? Like open source software is, you know, it is quite literally, you know, it's the technological equivalent of free speech.
1:26:36
which means it's the technological equivalent of free thought. And it is the way that the software industry has developed to be able to build many of the most critical components of the modern technological world.
1:26:45
And then Ben, as you said earlier, to be able to secure those and to be able to have those actually be safe and reliable. And then to have the transparency that we've talked about so that you know how they work and how they're making decisions.
1:26:58
And then to your last point also, so that you can have customized AI in many different environments. So you don't end up with a world where you just have one or a couple AIs, but you actually have a diversity of AIs with lots of different points of view and lots of different capabilities.
1:27:11
And so the open source fight is actually at the core of this.
1:27:15
And, and of course, the reason why, you know, the sort of, you know, sort of people with an eye towards monopoly or cartel want to ban this as open source is a tremendous threat to monopoly or cartel, like, you know, in many ways is a guarantee that monopoly or cartel can't last.
1:27:28
But it is absolutely 100%, you know, required for the, you know, for the furtherance of monopoly. Number one, a vibrant private sector. Number two, a vibrant startup sector.
1:27:36
And then right back to the academia point, like without open source, then at that point, you know, university, college kids are just not going to be able to, they're not even going to be able to learn how the technology works. They're just going to be like completely boxed out.
1:27:48
And so a world where open source is banned is bad on so many fronts. It's just incredible to me that anybody's advocating for it, but it needs to be, I think it needs to be recognized as the threat that it is.
1:27:57
Yeah. And on the note, you know, it was such a funny dialogue between you and he. So, like, I'll just give you a quick summary of it. Basically, you know, he was arguing for closed source, you for open source. His core argument was this is the Manhattan Project, and therefore we can't let anybody know the secrets.
1:28:19
And you countered that by saying, well, if this is in fact the Manhattan Project, then it's like, you know, it's the open AI team, you know, locked. In a remote location, do they screen all their employees very, very carefully? Is it airlocked? Is there super high security? Of course, none of that is close to true.
1:28:38
In fact, I'm quite sure they have Chinese nationals working there. Probably some are spies for the Chinese government. There's no any kind of strong security at OpenAI or at Google or at any of these places. anywhere near the Manhattan Project, which is where they built a whole city that nobody knew about. So they couldn't get into it.
1:29:00
And, you know, once you caught him in that, he said nothing. And then he says back, well, you know, it costs billions of dollars to train these models. You just want to give that away? Is that good? You know, is that good economics?
1:29:11
Like that was his like final counterpoint to you, which basically said, oh yeah, I'm trying to preserve a monopoly here. Like, what are you doing? I'm an investor. And I think that's true for all these arguments.
1:29:23
Well, the kicker, you know, the kicker, Ben, to that story, the kicker to that is three days later, the Justice Department indicted a Chinese national Google employee who stole Google's next generation AI chip designs, which is quite literally the family tools.
1:29:40
uh for an ai program it's you know it's the equivalent of stealing the you know if you stretch the metaphor the equivalent of stealing the design for the bomb um and that google employee um uh took that those chips downloaded them and took them to china and by definition you know my definition that means took them to the chinese government because there's no distinction in china uh between the private sector and the government um it's an integrated thing um the government owns and controls everything um and so you know 100 100 guaranteed that that went straight to the chinese government chinese military
1:30:08
And Google, which, you know, like, you know, Google has like a big information security team and all the rest of it. Google did not realize, according to the indictment, Google did not realize that that engineer had been in China for six months.
1:30:20
Yeah. Amazing. Well, hold on. It gets better. It gets better. This is the same Google with the same CEO who refused to sell Google proprietary AI technology to the U.S. Department of Defense.
1:30:35
So they're supplying China with AI and not supplying the U.S., which just goes back to, look, if it's not open source, we're never going to compete. Like, yeah, we've lost the future of the world right here, which is why, you know, it's the single most important AI issue for sure.
1:30:55
Yeah. And and you're not going to lock this stuff up like you're not going to lock it up. Nobody's locking it up. It's not locked up. These companies are security Swiss cheese. And like, you know, you're not going to you know, and you'd have a debate about the tactical relevance of chip embargoes and so forth.
1:31:09
But like you're you're you're not the horse has left the barn on this, not least because these companies are without a doubt riddled with with with foreign assets and they're very easy to penetrate. Um, and so we, we, we just have to be like, I would say very realistic about the actual state of play here.
1:31:24
Um, and, and, and we have to, we have to, we have to play in reality and we have to, we have to play in reality. We have to win in reality. Um, and the, as we need innovation, we need competition. We need free thought. We need free speech.
1:31:33
We need, we need to embrace the virtues of our system. Um, and, and, and not shut ourselves down in the face of, uh, in the face of, uh, the, uh, you know, the, the conflicts that are, that are coming. Another one. Andreas asks, why are USVCs so much more engaged in politics and policy than their global counterparts?
1:31:51
And I really appreciate that question because it basically, if that's the question, then it means that, boy, VCs outside the US must not be engaged at all because USVCs are barely engaged.
1:32:03
Yeah.
1:32:04
And then what do you believe the impact of this is on both the VC ecosystem and society in general? And then directly related question, Vincent asks, are European AI companies becoming less interesting investment targets for US-based VCs due to the strict and predictably unpredictable regulatory landscape in Europe?
1:32:21
Would you advise early stage European AI companies to consider relocating to the US as a result?
1:32:26
Great question. Well, look, I think that it kind of goes back to a little of what you said earlier, which is, you know, in startup world, like there's, you know, in the West, there's the United States and then there's everywhere else. And the United States is kind of bigger than everywhere else combined.
1:32:45
And, you know, so it's natural. And look, you know, in these kind of political things, it kind of starts with, the leader. And, you know, U.S. is the leader in VC. We feel like we're the leaders in U.S. VC, so we need to go to Washington. Until we go, you know, nobody's going.
1:33:07
And so that's a lot of the reason why we started things. Wow, on European regulatory policy, look, it's I think generally regulatory policy is likely to dictate where you can build these companies. We've seen some interesting things. France turns out to be leading a revolution in Europe on AI regulatory.
1:33:34
where they're basically telling the EU to pound sand, you know, and a large reason because they have a company there, Mistral, and, you know, it's a national jewel for the country, and they don't want to give it up because, you know, the EU has some crazy safetyism, you know, thing going on there.
1:33:53
Yeah, and I would also note France also, of course, is playing the same role with nuclear policy in Europe.
1:33:57
Yeah, yeah. They're the one country in Europe. They're the cleanest country, probably one of the cleanest countries in the world as a result.
1:34:04
Right, but have been staunchly pro-nuclear and trying to hold off, I think in a lot of ways, sort of attempts throughout the rest of Europe and especially from Germany to basically ban nuclear, civilian nuclear power.
1:34:15
Yeah, and the UK has sort of been flip-flopping on AI policy and we'll see where they come out. Yeah. Yeah, Brussels has been ridiculous as they've been on almost everything.
1:34:26
Yeah, the big thing I think I note here is there's a really big philosophical distinction. I think it's rooted actually in the difference between, traditionally it's been called, I think, the sort of Anglo-American kind of approach to law and then the continental European approach.
1:34:39
And I forget these, it's like, I forget the terms for it, but the legal...
1:34:44
There's like common law and then, yeah.
1:34:47
I think it's civil law. Yeah, yes. So the difference basically is that which is not outlawed is legal. Right. Or that which is specifically legal is legal. And anything that's not explicitly legal is outlawed. Right. In other words, like by default, do you have freedom and then you impose the law to have constraints?
1:35:09
Or by default, do you have no ability to do anything and then the law enables you to do things? And these are sort of, this is like a fundamental, like philosophical, legal, political distinction.
1:35:21
And then this shows up in a lot of these policy issues with this idea called the precautionary principle, which is sort of the rewording of the sort of traditional European approach, which is basically the precautionary principle says new technologies should not be allowed to be fielded until they are proven to be harmless.
1:35:38
And of course, the precautionary principle very specifically is sort of a hallmark of the European approach to regulation and increasingly by the US approach. And specifically, its origin, it was actually sort of described in that way and given that name actually by the German Greens in the 1970s as a means to ban civilian nuclear power.
1:36:01
By the way, with just catastrophic results. And we could spend a lot of time on that. But I think everybody at this point agrees, including the Germans, increasingly agree that that was a big mistake.
1:36:10
Among other things, has led to basically Europe funding Russia's invasion of Ukraine through the need for imported energy because they keep shutting down their nuclear plants. And so just like a sort of a catastrophic decision. But the precautionary principle has become, like I would say, extremely trendy. Like it's one of these things, like it sounds great, right?
1:36:28
It's like, well, why would you want anything to be released in the world if it's not proved to be harmless? Like how can you possibly be in support of anything that's going to cause harm? But the obvious problem with that is with that principle, you could have never deployed technology such as fire, electric power,
1:36:44
internal combustion, engines, cars, airplanes, the computer, right? Like every single piece of technology we have that powers modern day civilization has some way in which it can be used to hurt people. Every single one. Technologies are double-edged swords. You can use fire to protect your village or to attack the neighboring village.
1:37:05
These things can be used in both different ways. Basically, if we had applied the precautionary principle historically, we would still be living in mud huts. We would be just absolutely miserable. The idea of imposing the precautionary principle today, if you're coming from an Anglo-American perspective or from a freedom to innovate perspective, that's just incredibly horrifying.
1:37:28
It's basically guaranteed to stall out progress. This is very much the mentality of the EU bureaucrats in particular, and this is the mentality behind a lot of their recent legislation on technology issues. France does seem to be the main counterweight against this in Europe.
1:37:46
Ben, to your point, the UK has been a counterweight in some areas, but UK also has, like I would say, they've received a full dose of this programming.
1:37:53
Yeah, they have that tendency.
1:37:56
Yeah. And they've been in AI in particular, I think they've been on the wrong side of that, which hopefully they'll reconsider. So again, this is one of these things like this, this is a really, really important issue. And just the surface level thing of like, okay, this technology might be able to be used for some harmful purpose.
1:38:10
Like that, if that is allowed to be the end of the discussion, like we are never going to, nothing new is ever going to happen in the world. Like that will cause us ultimately to stall out completely. Yeah.
1:38:18
Um, and, and then, you know, if, if we stall out that, that will over time lead to regression and, and like literally, you know, I mean, this is happening, like the power is going out, like, you know, German society, German, German society, German, uh, industrial companies are shutting down because they can't afford the power that's resulted from this, this, uh, you know, kind of the imposition of this policy in the energy sector.
1:38:36
Um, and so this is a very, very, very important thing. I think the EU bureaucracy is lost on this. And so I think it's going to be up to the individual countries, um, to directly confront this if they want to.
1:38:47
Anyway, so I really applaud what France has done, and I hope more European countries join them in kind of being on the right side on this.
1:38:53
Yeah, you know, it always is funny to me to hear the EU and like the economists and these kinds of things say, oh, the EU may not be the leader in innovation, but we're the leaders in regulation. And I'm like, well, you realize those go together. They're like one is a function of the other.
1:39:10
Okay, good. And then let's do one more global question. Lap Gong Leong asks, are there any other countries that could be receptive to techno-optimism? For example, could Britain, Argentina, or Japan be ideal targets for our message and mission?
1:39:24
Yes. So we'll look for work on that in Britain. And look, we've got some pretty good reception from the UK government. There's a lot of very, very smart people there. We're working with them, you know, tightly on... their AI and crypto efforts, and we're hoping that's the case.
1:39:46
Japan, having spent a lot of time there, they've obviously shown that capability over time, and then there's a lot about the way Japanese society works that holds them back from that at times as well.
1:40:05
you know, without getting into all the specifics, there's, you know, they have a very, I would just say, unusual and unique culture that has a great deference for the old way of doing things, which sometimes makes it hard to kind of promote the new way of doing things.
1:40:20
I also think, you know, around the world, you know, the Middle East is very, very kind of subject and kind of on board with techno-optimism. The UAE,
1:40:33
Saudi, Israel, of course, many countries out there are very excited about these kinds of ideas and taking the world forward and just creating a better world through technology, which I think that, look, with our population growth, if we don't have a better world through technology, we're going to have a worse world without technology.
1:40:53
I think that's very obvious. So it's a very compelling message. And oh, by the way, South America, I should say also, there are a lot of countries who are really embracing techno-optimism now in South America, and some great new leadership there that's pushing that.
1:41:14
Yeah, I would also say if you look at the polling on this, what I think you find is what you could describe as the younger countries are more enthusiastic about technology. And I don't mean younger here literally of like when they were formed, but I mean two things.
1:41:25
One is how recently they've kind of emerged into what we would consider to be modernity. And so, you know, for example, to embrace concepts like democracy or free market capitalism or innovation generally. you know, or global trade and so forth.
1:41:37
And then the other is just quite simply the number of the demographics, you know, the countries with a larger number of people. And those are often by the way the same countries, right? They have the reverse demographic pyramid we have where they actually have a lot of young people.
1:41:51
And young people are both, you know, young people both need economic opportunity and are very fired up about new ideas.
1:41:56
Yeah, by the way, this is true in Africa as well. In many African countries, you know, Nigeria, Rwanda, Ghana, they were there. Techno-optimism, I think, has taken hold in a real way. Some of them need governance improvements, but they definitely also have young populations. Saudi, I think 70% of the population is under 30.
1:42:17
So just to your point, very, very, very hopeful in those areas.
1:42:26
Ben, Goltra asks, do you think the lobbying efforts by good faith American crypto firms will be able to move the needle politically in the next few years? What areas make you optimistic as it relates to American crypto regulation? Crypto, blockchain, Web3.
1:42:38
Yeah, so I think that I'm as hopeful as I've ever been. So there's a bunch of things that have been really positive. First of all, the SEC has lost I think five cases in a row. So, you know, like some of their like arbitrary, um, enforcement of things that aren't laws is not working.
1:42:58
Um, secondly, uh, you know, there was a bill that passed through the house, um, or the house financial services committee, uh, which is a very, I would say good bill on crypto regulation. And, you know, hopefully that will eventually pass the house and the Senate. Um, there's, uh,
1:43:19
You know, we've seen Wyoming, I think, adopt really good new laws around DAOs. And so there's some progress there. And then, you know, there's been, you know, we've been working really, really hard to educate members of Congress and the administration on kind of the value of the technology.
1:43:41
There are strong opponents to it, you know, as I mentioned earlier. And, you know, that continues to be worrisome. But I think we're making great progress. And the Fair Shake Pack has done just a tremendous job of, you know, kind of backing pro-crypto candidates.
1:44:01
And with great success, there were six different races on Super Tuesday that they backed and all six won. So, you know, another good sign. Fantastic.
1:44:13
I had a couple other topics here quickly to get under the wire. So Father Time asks, can you give us your thoughts on the recent TikTok legislation? If passed, what does this mean for big tech going forward? And so I'll just give a quick swing at that. So the TikTok legislation being proposed by the U.S.
1:44:28
Congress and currently being taken up in the Senate, which, by the way, the president Biden has already said he'll sign it if the Senate and the House pass it. This is legislation that would require a divestment of TikTok from its Chinese parent company, ByteDance.
1:44:43
TikTok would have to be a purely American company or would have to be owned by a purely American company. Then failing that, it would be a ban of TikTok in the US.
1:44:53
This bill is a great example of the bipartisan dynamic in DC right now on the topic of China, which is this bill is being enthusiastically supported by the majority of politicians on both sides of the aisle.
1:45:05
I think it passed out of its committee like 50 to zero, which, you know, is basically like it's impossible to get anybody in D.C. to agree on anything right now, except basically basically this. So this is like super bipartisan. And then it's, you know, the head of that committee is a Republican, Mike Gallagher.
1:45:21
And, you know, he immediately and he worked in a bipartisan way with his committee members. But. The Democratic White House immediately endorsed his bill. So this bill has serious momentum. The Senate is taking it up right now. They're likely to modify it in some way, but it seems reasonably likely to pass based on what we can see.
1:45:40
You know, I would say, like I said, overwhelmingly bipartisan support. And, you know, look, the argument for the ban is, I would say, a couple different ways.
1:45:48
The divestment of the ban, number one, is just like, you know, an app on Americans, on the phones of a large percent of Americans with surveillance and potential propaganda kind of aspects of that, you know, certainly has people in Washington concerned. And then, quite frankly, there's an underlying industrial, you know, dynamic, which is, you know, the U.S.
1:46:08
Internet companies can't operate in China. So there's a sort of an unfair asymmetry underneath this that really undercuts, I think, a lot of the arguments from ByteDance.
1:46:17
It has been striking to see that there are actually opponents of this bill who have emerged, and I would describe on sort of the further to the right and further to the left in their respective parties. And those folks, and I won't go through detail, but those folks make a variety of arguments
1:46:34
And let me characterize the surface level.
1:46:39
I think further on the left, I think that there are people who think that, especially kind of further left, Congress people who feel like TikTok is actually a really important and vital messaging system for them to be able to use with their constituents who tend to be younger and very internet centric.
1:46:53
And so there's that, which is interesting. But then further on the right, there is a lot, and our friend David Sachs, for example, might be an example of this, There are a fair number of people who are very worried that the U.S.
1:47:06
government is so prone to abuse any regulatory capability with respect to tech, and especially with respect to censorship, that basically if you hand the U.S.
1:47:14
government any new regulatory authority or legal authority at all to come down on tech, it will inevitably be used not just against the Chinese company, but it will also then be used against the American companies. And so there's some drama surfacing around this, and we'll see whether the opponents can...
1:47:29
you know, can kind of kind of pull it through. You know, look, quite frankly, I, you know, I, you know, without coming down particularly on, like, I think there's one of those cases where it's actually like excellent arguments, like on all three sides. Like, I think there are like very legitimate questions here.
1:47:43
And so, you know, I think it's great that the issue is being confronted. But I think it's also great that the, you know, the arguments have surfaced and that we're going to, you know, hopefully figure out the right thing to do. A couple of closing things. Close on, let's see, hopefully a semi-optimistic note.
1:47:58
So John Potter asks, how do you most effectively find common ground with groups and interests that you benefit from working with, but with which you are usually opposed ideologically or otherwise?
1:48:09
I mean, I think this is, you know, there's this term in Washington, common ground. And I think that, you know, you always want to start by finding the common ground, because I'll tell you something in politics generally, is most people have the same intention. You know, like in Washington, in fact, people want life to be fair.
1:48:35
You know, they don't want people to go hungry. They want citizens to be safe but have plenty of opportunity. There's a lot of common ground that the differences lie not in the intent, but how you get there, what is the right policy to achieve the goal.
1:48:56
I think it's always important to start with the goal and then work our way through why we think our policy position is correct. You know, like, we don't really have a lot of disagreements on stated intent, at least. I mean, I think there are some intentions that are very difficult in Washington, you know, like the...
1:49:23
you know, the intention to kind of control the financial system is, you know, from the government or nationalize the banks or kind of achieve the equivalent of nationalizing the banks is, you know, when you have that intent, that's tough.
1:49:37
But like, if you start with, you know, most intentions are, I think, you know, shared between, you know, us and policymakers on both sides.
1:49:47
And then we'll close on this great question. Zach asks, would either of you ever consider running for office? And for fun, what would be your platform?
1:49:57
So I want, just because, look, you know, I think being a politician requires a certain kind of skill set and attitude and energy from certain things that I don't possess, unfortunately. Do you have a platform you would run on if you did run? Okay, yeah, let's hear your platform.
1:50:25
The American Dream. So I won't do it now, but I like to put up this chart that shows the change in prices in different sectors of the economy over time. And what you basically see is the price of like television sets and software and video games are like crashing hard, right?
1:50:40
In a way that's like great for consumers. I saw 75 inch flat screen ultra high def TVs now are down below $500. It's great. It's amazing. When technology is allowed to work its magic, prices crash in a way that's just great for consumers. And it's equivalent of a giant, basically when prices drop, it's equivalent of a raise.
1:50:59
So it makes human welfare a lot better. The three elements of the economy that are central to the American dream are healthcare, education, and housing. And so if you think about what does it mean to have the American dream, it means to be able to buy and own a home.
1:51:14
It means being able to send your kids to great schools, get a great education, to have a great life. And then it means great healthcare to be able to take care of yourself and your family. The prices on those are skyrocketing. They're just like straight to the moon.
1:51:27
Of course, those are the sectors that are the most controlled by the government, where there's the most subsidies for demand from the government, there's the most restrictions on supply from the government, and there is the most interference with the ability to field technology and startups.
1:51:43
And the result is we have an entire generation of kids who basically, I think, are quite rational in looking forward and basically saying, I'm never going to be able to achieve the American dream. I'm never going to be able to own a home.
1:51:53
I'm never going to be able to get a good education or send my kids to good education. I'm not going to be able to get good health care. Basically, I'm not going to be able to live the life that my parents lived or my grandparents lived.
1:52:02
And I'm not going to be I'm not going to be able to fundamentally form a family, provide for my kids. And I think that's the I think that's my opinion. That's the underlying theme to kind of what has gone wrong sort of socially, politically, psychologically in the country.
1:52:16
That's what's led to this sort of intense level of pessimism. That's what's led to sort of this attraction, you know, kind of very zero sum politics to, you know, recrimination over over over optimism and building. And so I would confront that absolutely directly.
1:52:31
And then of course, I would point out that I don't think anybody in Washington is doing that right now. So either I would win because I'm the only one saying it out loud or I would lose because nobody cares.
1:52:41
But I think it would, I've always wondered whether that actually would, both on the substance and on the message, whether that would be the right platform.
1:52:51
Yeah, no, it would certainly be the thing to do. It's the thing, it's very complex in that, you know, healthcare policy is largely national, but education policy and housing policy has also got a very large local component. So it would be a complicated set of policies that you'd have to enforce.
1:53:13
We still have a ton of questions, so we may do part two on this at some point, but we really appreciate your time and attention and we will see you soon.
1:53:19
Okay, thank you.

Topic Index (for deep-dive generation)

1. Big tech vs little tech divergence — interests have fully separated; conflating them is a category error 2. Safety theater as monopoly capture — big tech AI lobbying; the Biden EO smuggle 3. Regulating uses, not math — every harmful AI use is already illegal 4. The leapfrog dynamic — AI as commodity is big tech's actual nightmare 5. Crypto / blockchain as decentralization layer — training, inference, data marketplaces, deepfake provenance 6. The Vinod Khosla / Manhattan Project debate — closed source as security theater 7. Cold War 2.0 with China — two AI stacks, decentralization as strategic edge 8. The Anglo-American vs Continental legal traditions — precautionary principle dissected 9. Meta's open-source bet as Android-style strategy — Llama as anti-Apple play 10. The American Dream platform — where tech operates prices crash, where government dominates prices explode 11. University AI capacity collapse — without decentralization, academia is boxed out 12. TikTok bill bipartisan dynamics — left/right opposition reasoning 13. VC engagement asymmetry — why US VCs are barely engaged and global VCs not at all 14. France as European counterweight — Mistral, nuclear, the Paris exception 15. Round-tripping investment dynamics — big tech investing in startups conditional on GPU/cloud purchases
